content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660              322-5901              322-6441              322-6444

March 23, 1982


Jeffrey Gunther
Assistant Attorney General
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Gunther:

     We are writing in response to your request for assistance
as attorney for the California Horse Racing Board. A question
has been raised as to a possible conflict of interest under
Government Code Section 87100-1/ on the part of Board member
Richard Groulx with respect to Hollywood Park's application
for a license for the 1982 racing meetings. You anticipate
being asked to advise the Board on this issue at its next
meeting.

     Mr. Papiano has made a written submission to the Commission,
and I have spoken to Mr. Groulx and to Mr. Neil Papiano, attorney
for Hollywood Park, as well as to yourself. Our advice is based
on the information we have received from all parties. The facts,
as we understand them, are as follows:

     Mr. Groulx is the Executive Secretary of the Alameda County
Central Labor Council of the AFL-CIO, a position he has held since
1970. The Alameda Council includes 150 unions, which represent
approximately 62,000 employees. Six of these unions represent race track employees.

     The Alameda Council is supported financially by its member
unions, which contribute 35 cents for each union member on an annual
basis. The amount each union contributes to the Alameda Central
Council is computed solely on this per capita basis. The contribution
does not depend on the wages of the union members.

_________________________________
1/ All statutory references made are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

Jeffrey Gunther
March 23, 1982
Page Two


     The contract between the unions which represent race
track employees and the tracks will expire in December 1982.
Negotiations have not yet commenced on the new contracts.
At the February 19, 1982, meeting of the Board, a union
representative requested that the Board delay action on
Hollywood Park's application for a license for the 1982
racing meeting.  The Board voted unanimously to grant a
license to Hollywood Park to race the dates of April 23,
1982 through July 19, 1982, as a split meeting and postponed
the vote on a license for the dates between November 3, 1982
and December 23, 1982, to the March 26, 1982 Board meeting.
At the March 26 meeting the union is expected to renew its
request to postpone granting the license to Hollywood Park
until after a labor contract has been signed.

     In 1976 and 1979, negotiations between the unions and
the tracks broke down and the unions went out on strike.
Both times Mr. Groulx participated in the strike settlement
negotiations as executive secretary of the Alameda County
Central Labor Council.  Mr. Groulx has not participated in
any union negotiations with Hollywood Park (or with any of
the other tracks) since his appointment to the Board.

     Mr. Groulx and Mr. Papiano are not in agreement as to
how to characterize Mr. Groulx's possible participation in
the contract negotiations which are expected to take place
between the unions and the tracks in 1982. According to
Mr. Groulx, he will not become involved in statewide negotia-
tions or in local negotiations with any tracks outside Alameda
County.  He said he might become involved in local negotiations
with tracks in Alameda (Golden Gate Park and Pleasanton) if
the negotiations reached an impasse and he were asked to
come in as a "partisan mediator" to try to avert a strike.
He also said that if there were a strike by any AFL-CIO
union at any track in the state, the Alameda Council would,
upon request, probably impose strike sanctions (e.g., help
set up picket lines).  On the other hand, Mr. Papiano is
clearly of the belief that Mr. Groulx will play a significant
role in any statewide negotiations between the unions and
the tracks.

     Since the Commission, in issuing advice or opinions,
does not act as a factfinder, we have not attempted to resolve
this factual dispute.  My advice only attempts to explain
the relevant legal standards under the Political Reform Act.
The application of the law to the specific facts of this

Jeffrey Gunther
March 23, 1982
Page Three

dispute is, in the final analysis, the responsibility of
Mr. Groulx.

Under the Political Reform Act, Mr. Groulx must disqualify
himself from making or participating in any governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest. Section 87100. An official has a financial
interest in a decision within the meaning of the Act if
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, on any business entity or real
property in which the official has an investment of $1,000
or more, or on any source of income of $250 or more to the
official within the previous 12 months. Section 87103.
Since the Alameda County Central Labor Council is a source
of income of over $250 to Mr. Groulx, he must disqualify
himself from decisions which will foreseeably have a material
financial effect on the Council.

The Commission has interpreted the term "material financial
effect" by regulation. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.
Applying this regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3),
a decision of the Board (in which Mr. Groulx participated)
would have a material financial effect on the Alameda Central
Labor Council if:

(a) The effect of the decision would be to directly
increase or decrease the income received by Mr. Groulx from
the Council by $100 or more;

(b) There is a "nexus" between the governmental decision
and the purpose for which Mr. Groulx receives his income
from the Council; or

(c) The decision will have a "significant effect" on
the Council.

The first test - that the decision increase or decrease
Mr. Groulx's income from the Council by $100 or more - does
not appear to be applicable.

The second test - whether there is a "nexus" between
the government decision and Mr. Groulx's employment by the
Council - should be considered. Stated as simply as possible,
there is a nexus between a government decision and the purpose
of an official's employment whenever the official, by an

Jeffrey Gunther
March 23, 1982
Page Four


official act, attempts to accomplish what he is paid to do
as a non-governmental employee.2/ In the case of Mr. Groulx,
this means that he cannot vote on or participate in decisions
in his capacity as a Board member if the decisions involve
matters in which he is participating, is likely to participate,
or would ordinarily be expected to participate in his capacity
as executive secretary of the Council.

     For example, if, as executive secretary of the Alameda
Council, Mr. Groulx were to conduct or participate in contract
negotiations between a member union and a racetrack, he
could not also, as a Board member, vote on or participate in
decisions on matters which were the subject of those negotiations,
or in decisions which would foreseeably have a significant
effect on the course or outcome of those negotiations.
Thus, if it is foreseeable that Mr. Groulx in his capacity
as executive secretary of the Alameda Central Labor Council,
will participate in the labor negotiations between the unions
and Hollywood Park, he may not, in his capacity as a Board
member, participate in a decision on the union's request to
defer consideration of Hollywood Park's application until
after the contract is negotiated.  This advice is based on
the assumption that deferring the license under such conditions
would affect the labor negotiations.  To take another example,
if there were to be a strike, and if Mr. Groulx, as executive
secretary of the Council, were to play a role in the imposition
of enforcement of strike sanctions, he would have to disqualify
himself from Board decisions which could foreseeably affect
the duration or effectiveness of the strike.

     After speaking to both Mr. Groulx and to Mr. Papiano,
we do not feel confident that we fully understand what role,
if any, Mr. Groulx might play or will play as executive
secretary of the Council with respect to the upcoming contract
negotiations between the unions and the racetracks.  Therefore,
we can only advise you that if he is presently involved, in
his capacity as executive secretary, in preparing for these
negotiations, or if it is foreseeable that he will participate
in the negotiations, a nexus would exist between his role as
executive secretary of the Council and any decisions the
Board is considering which are intended to influence the
negotiations.  Whether or not such a nexus exists in connection
with the issue of Hollywood Park's license, depends on the

_______________________________
     2/ I have enclosed a copy of staff advice to Linda
Best interpreting the "nexus" test in another situation.

Jeffrey Gunther
March 23, 1982
Page Five


nature, if any, of Mr. Groulx's present or anticipated role
as executive secretary of the Alameda Council in the negotiations
between Hollywood Park and the unions. This is, ultimately,
a factual question which the Commission is not in a position
to decide on the basis of the presently available information.

The third test - whether a decision has a "significant
effect" on the Council - does not appear to be relevant on
the basis of the information which we have received. The
significant effect referred to in this test is a financial
effect. Before Mr. Groulx could be disqualified on the
basis of this test, it would be necessary to show that a
Board decision would foreseeably affect the assets or income
of the Council to a significant degree. As we understand
the situation, the Council is supported by dues, assessed on
a per capita basis, from its member unions. There is no
indication that the terms of any contract which is negotiated
would foreseeably affect the amount the Council would receive
from any member union. Therefore, a Board decision which
might influence the outcome of the negotiations would probably
not have a foreseeable financial effect on the Council within
the meaning of this test. There may, of course, be some
Board decisions which could affect the Council financially.
For example, if the Council (rather than the individual
unions) maintains a strike fund, a Board decision which
would foreseeably determine whether the Council would have
(or not have) to expend this strike fund could have a significant
financial effect on the Council.

As you can see from the foregoing analysis, there may
be decisions from which, using the nexus test, Mr. Groulx
would be required to disqualify himself. As you can also
see from the analysis, Mr. Groulx does not have to disqualify
himself from a decision which may have an effect on an AFL-
CIO affiliated union, unless (a) there is a nexus between
the particular decision in question and his role as executive
secretary of the Alameda Council, or (b) the decision will
have an identifiable, significant financial impact on the
Alameda Council.

We would like to point out that tests described above
for disqualification under the Act should be applied whenever
there is a suggestion that a Board decision may affect a
source of income of any Board member. In addition, a decision
which may foreseeably have a material financial effect on an
investment interest or real property interest of a Board
member, including a business venture of a board member, may
also require disqualification.

Jeffrey Gunther
March 23, 1982
Page Six


     Finally, we should point out that there are certain
activities which the Political Reform Act does not regulate,
even though they may, in common parlance, be referred to as
conflicts of interest. For example, the fact that a public
official has demonstrated a strong ideological bias on certain
issues does not mean that the official has to disqualify
himself from decisions relating to those issues under the
Act. Similarly, the fact that an official has, in the past,
been an advocate for one party to a dispute before a govern-
mental agency, does not necessarily mean that he must disqualify
himself from decisions affecting similar disputes in the
present, unless there is currently a nexus between the official's
employment and the issues involved in the dispute. Finally,
the fact that an official is employed by a trade association,
or a central labor council, does not mean that the official
has to disqualify himself from all decisions affecting the
members of the association or the council. Disqualification
is required only when the nexus test is satisfied, or when
the decision relating to the member will also foreseeably
have an identifiable significant financial effect on the
association or council.3/

     We hope that this analysis of the relevant law has been
helpful to you. This advice has been fairly general, due in
large part to my uncertainty about the specific factual
situation. However, if an individual Board member requests
our advice and provides us with all the relevant facts, we
can give more specific advice. A Board member who makes
such a request and relies on our advice can receive significant
protections from charges of conflicts of interest under the
Political Reform Act. Section 83114.

     If you have any further questions about the Political
Reform Act, I will be happy to discuss them with you.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

BAM:km

_____________________________

     3/ This issue is also discussed in the Linda Best
letters.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether California Horse Racing Board member Richard Groulx has a disqualifying conflict of interest under Government Code Section 87100 regarding Hollywood Park's license application, given his role as Executive Secretary of the Alameda County Central Labor Council with upcoming union contract negotiations with racetracks.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mr. Groulx must disqualify himself from decisions involving Hollywood Park's license if he will foreseeably participate in labor negotiations between unions and Hollywood Park as executive secretary of the Alameda Council, applying the 'nexus' test between his governmental decision and his employment purpose; however, the FPPC cannot definitively determine whether such a nexus exists based on available information and notes this is ultimately a factual question requiring Mr. Groulx's own assessment.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document has no explicit labeled QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections but contains clear question and conclusion material within the body text. Letter is dated March 23, 1982, addressed to Assistant Attorney General Jeffrey Gunther. Advice is conditional and fact-dependent, with FPPC noting it cannot act as factfinder on disputed factual claims about Groulx's anticipated role in negotiations.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether California Horse Racing Board member Richard Groulx has a disqualifying conflict of interest under Government Code Section 87100 regarding Hollywood Park's license application, given his role as Executive Secretary of the Alameda County Central Labor Council with upcoming union contract negotiations with racetracks.

CONCLUSION: Mr. Groulx must disqualify himself from decisions involving Hollywood Park's license if he will foreseeably participate in labor negotiations between unions and Hollywood Park as executive secretary of the Alameda Council, applying the 'nexus' test between his governmental decision and his employment purpose; however, the FPPC cannot definitively determine whether such a nexus exists based on available information and notes this is ultimately a factual question requiring Mr. Groulx's own assessment.

embedding.summary: FPPC provides legal guidance to the California Horse Racing Board regarding potential conflict of interest disqualification requirements for Board member Richard Groulx based on his dual roles as Board member and Executive Secretary of the Alameda County Central Labor Council amid pending union contract negotiations with racetracks. The advice explains the materiality standards and nexus test under the Political Reform Act and concludes that disqualification depends on whether Groulx will foreseeably participate in labor negotiations.
